title: Is the American Dream Still Achievable?
subtitle: A national analytics view of income growth, cost pressure, wage dispersion, and the widening ownership threshold from 2000 to 2024.

sidebar.label: Cost of Living
sidebar.summary: Income versus major household cost categories.

sidebar.label: Industry Divide
sidebar.summary: Compare a 2024-based 3-year forecast with actual 2024 wage leaders and laggards.

sidebar.label: Housing Burden
sidebar.summary: Monthly burden stayed tighter than ownership access.

sidebar.label: Homeownership
sidebar.summary: Construction costs outran wages and income.

verdict.label: Mixed
verdict.summary: Broad income growth outpaced overall inflation, but ownership access and mobility-linked categories moved against typical workers.

summaryCards.label: Primary signal
summaryCards.note: Per-capita income reached 239.7 versus 167.5 for overall PCE.

summaryCards.label: Pressure point
summaryCards.note: Higher education and the housing-related price indexes were the weakest affordability readings in 2024.

summaryCards.label: Ownership read
summaryCards.note: Single-family construction costs reached 255.6, far above income growth.

kpis.label: Dashboard verdict
kpis.note: Day-to-day consumption held up better than ownership entry.

insightCards.title: Aggregate affordability still exists
insightCards.body: National income outpaced broad PCE growth, so the dashboard should not claim a universal collapse in purchasing power.

insightCards.title: Mobility-linked categories did worse
insightCards.body: The weakest affordability readings come from higher education and housing-related price indexes, not groceries or aggregate spending.

leaders.label: Funds, trusts, and other financial vehicles

laggards.label: Motor vehicles, bodies and trailers, and parts
Motion picture and sound recording industries

leaders.label: Funds, trusts, and other financial vehicles

summary.title: Macro averages hide labor-market sorting
summary.body: The strongest indexed wage gains are concentrated in information, finance, and professional services rather than across the full job market.

summary.title: Growth gap is smaller than level gap
summary.body: Degree-heavy and non-degree-heavy buckets both grew, but the 2024 wage-level spread remains much larger than the index spread.

summary.title: Monthly burden stayed narrower than the public narrative
summary.body: Housing and utility spending as a share of personal income stayed within a tighter range than the ownership story would suggest.

summary.title: Renting and buying are different questions
summary.body: Tenant rent tracked income more closely than construction costs did, so entry into ownership is the sharper affordability break.

summary.title: Ownership is where the dream fractures
summary.body: Within the dashboard's broad housing-services affordability measure, 2024 sits at 113.1, while single-family construction costs rose much faster than income and better capture the ownership-entry squeeze.

summary.title: Supply context matters
summary.body: Residential investment share shows a boom, collapse, and partial recovery pattern, which helps explain why ownership access feels structurally constrained.

sources: All values are drawn from CSVs in `data/cleaned`, using the same BEA-backed repo datasets already prepared for analysis.
Income is constructed as total personal income divided by total population from `cleaned_income_with_state.csv`.
Indexed charts rebase each series to 2000 = 100 so growth rates can be compared directly; several widgets use BEA price indexes rather than market-price series.
Predictive ranking export is optional; without it, the dashboard falls back to repo-derived descriptive summaries.
Industry wage-level rankings use actual 2024 values from `annual_wages_per_FTE_by_industry.csv`, collapsed to unique industry labels before display.
caveats: This is a national and industry-level dashboard. It does not model taxes, debt, household composition, or local housing markets.
The state widget is context only. It shows per-capita income dispersion, not a full state affordability model.
No external APIs, web-fetched figures, home-price data, or mortgage assumptions are introduced in this dashboard.
Run `python scripts/export_dashboard_rankings.py` to refresh predictive ranking panels.
